sentences.text: The cat sat on the mat because it was tired.

interpretations.0: "The" looks ahead strongly, anchoring the noun phrase — it attends mainly to itself and nearby words.
interpretations.1: "cat" is the subject and draws strong attention. Notice how "it" (index 7) attends back here — the model has learned 'it' refers to the cat.
interpretations.2: "sat" is the main verb and attends to the subject "cat" — subject–verb agreement in action.
interpretations.3: "on" is a preposition and looks strongly at the following noun "mat" to form the prepositional phrase.
interpretations.4: The second "the" attends back to the first article, tracking parallel structure in the sentence.
interpretations.5: "mat" attends to itself and "the" — it's the object of the preposition phrase.
interpretations.6: "because" is a connector. It attends to itself and bridges to "it" and "tired" — setting up the causal clause.
interpretations.7: "it" is ambiguous — watch it attend strongly to "cat", not "mat". The model resolves this pronoun correctly based on what makes semantic sense as the tired entity.
interpretations.8: "was" is the auxiliary verb and attends to "it" (its subject) and "tired" (its complement).
interpretations.9: "tired" is the predicate adjective and attends back to the subject "cat" via "it" — a long-range dependency.

sentences.text: The nurse told the doctor that she was ready.

interpretations.0: "The" attends to the noun it introduces — "nurse".
interpretations.1: "nurse" is the subject. Notice the model doesn't yet know who "she" refers to.
interpretations.2: "told" attends to its subject "nurse" and its object "doctor" — the verb anchors the main clause.
interpretations.3: The second "the" attends to the first "the" and introduces "doctor".
interpretations.4: "doctor" is the indirect object. "she" attends to both "nurse" and "doctor" — genuine ambiguity here.
interpretations.5: "that" is a complementiser. It introduces the subordinate clause and attends to the verb "told".
interpretations.6: "she" is genuinely ambiguous — it attends to both "nurse" and "doctor". Without more context, the model cannot fully resolve this. This illustrates the limits of attention for social pronoun resolution.
interpretations.7: "was" attends to "she" (its subject) and links to "ready".
interpretations.8: "ready" attends back to "she", "nurse", and "doctor" — the model is tracking who is ready.

sentences.text: The keys which I left on the table near the door were missing.

interpretations.0: "The" introduces "keys" and anchors the main noun phrase.
interpretations.1: "keys" is the main subject, buried under a long relative clause. Watch how "were" attends strongly back to "keys" — essential for grammatical agreement.
interpretations.2: "which" is a relative pronoun. It attends to "keys" — establishing the referent of the relative clause.
interpretations.3: "I" is the subject of the embedded clause and attends to itself and the verb "left".
interpretations.4: "left" attends to its subject "I" and its object location — the table.
interpretations.5: "on" looks at "table" — locating the keys in the prepositional phrase.
interpretations.6: The third "the" introduces "table" and attends to prior articles.
interpretations.7: "table" is the location and attends back to "left" — confirming where the keys were left.
interpretations.8: "near" attends to both "table" and "door" — bridging the two locational phrases.
interpretations.9: The fourth "the" introduces "door" and attends to the article pattern in the sentence.
interpretations.10: "door" attends to "near" and "table" — providing spatial context.
interpretations.11: "were" is the main verb. It must attend to "keys" across the entire relative clause — a long-range dependency of 10 tokens.
interpretations.12: "missing" attends to "keys" and "were" — confirming that the keys are what is missing.

sentences.text: Edinburgh is to Scotland as Tokyo is to Japan.

interpretations.0: "Edinburgh" attends to itself and "Scotland" — the model has encoded the capital–country relationship.
interpretations.1: "is" links Edinburgh to the analogical structure and mirrors the second "is" in the sentence.
interpretations.2: "to" marks the relational structure — it attends to "Scotland", completing the analogy half.
interpretations.3: "Scotland" attends to "Edinburgh" strongly. It also attends to "Japan" — the parallel country. The model understands both are nations with capitals.
interpretations.4: "as" is the analogy connector. It attends to both halves symmetrically — key to recognising this as an analogy.
interpretations.5: "Tokyo" attends strongly to itself and to "Edinburgh" — the model recognises both are capitals, enabling analogical reasoning.
interpretations.6: The second "is" mirrors the first and attends to "Tokyo" and the structural parallel.
interpretations.7: The second "to" mirrors the first and attends to "Japan" — completing the second half of the analogy.
interpretations.8: "Japan" attends to "Tokyo" and "Scotland" — completing the four-way analogy: Edinburgh:Scotland :: Tokyo:Japan.
